text: Hello friends, today we are going to present a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy for carcinoma cecum.
This is a case of a forty-two year old female referred to us for further investigation for severe anemia.
She had received injectable iron recently.
subjected her for upper GI endoscopy and colonoscopy.
Upper GI endoscopy revealed normal.
The colonoscopy reveals an ulcerative proliferative growth at the ileocecal junction, characterized by irregular tissue appearance and potential obstruction.
The video shows an ulcerative proliferative growth located just proximal to the ileocecal junction, with visible tissue irregularities and ulceration.
The narrow band imaging reveals a detailed view of the ulcerative proliferative growth at the ileocecal junction, from which multiple biopsies are being taken.
and send for histopathological reports.
Colonic biopsy revealed well-differentiated adenocarcinoma.

The CT scan abdomen for this patient revealed focal thickening of cecum and ascending colon for a length of six point seven centimeters and it is fourteen mm in maximum thickness and there is an irregularity of underlying serosa and no significant luminal narrowing is detected.
but there is no appendix is visualized on CT scan abdomen.
There was no comorbidity, hemodynamically patient was stable, her hemoglobin was ten grams of gram percent and then she posted for laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.
The team who managed this case was Dr. Rahul Mahadhar myself, surgeon and endoscopist.
Dr. A.M. Arora was assisting surgeon. Dr. Dhanashree Mahadhar was a camera surgeon. Dr. Mrs. Sonia Naik was anesthetist. And Dr. Nirmal Thakur was physician to manage this case.

In the operation theatre setup, the surgeon stands on the left side of the patient, while the camera holder is positioned between the patient's legs. The patient is in a leg split position with reverse Trendelenburg positioning, commonly used for this colectomy procedure.
T-tube monitors are positioned on both sides of the patient at shoulder level, while the scrub nurse is stationed at the patient's right leg side.

The laparoscopic port setup for the right hemicolectomy includes a 10mm port at the suprapubic region, a 12mm port on the left side near the umbilicus for stapling, a 5mm port in the left subcostal region along the midclavicular line, a mirrored 5mm port on the right side in the right subcostal region at the midclavicular line, and a FIMM port in the right iliac fossa.

The surgeon lifts the right mesocolon and performs a dissection between the mesocolon and Gerota's fascia. Vessels are taken during the dissection, followed by supracolic dissection around the hepatic flexure. The lateral attachments of the ascending colon and cecum are then dissected.

The surgeon completes mobilization of the right side of the colon and performs a side-to-side ileocolic anastomosis. A cystoscopic procedure is conducted to insert a lighted ureteral stent on the right side.

The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with legs split, while the camera operator stands between the patient's legs. The small bowel loops are shifted to the left side, and the surgeon lifts the ileocolic vessel pedicle, exposing it for further dissection.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to create a dissection plane between the ileocolic vessels and the posterior mesocolon, extending towards the midline. The facial plane is dissected between the torus fascia and the Gerota's fascia.
The surgeon visualizes the ileocolic vein, encountering minor bleeding while creating the dissection plane. The ileocolic vessel is carefully dissected to manage the bleeding.

The surgeon identifies the origin of the ileocolic vessel, applying hemoclips and a ligar clip before using a ligature to cut the vessel. The dissection continues between the vessel and the artery, with lymphatic tissue being addressed. The incision is extended laterally on the mesentery to create a large fascial plane between the right colon mesentery and the posterior fascia.

The surgeon dissects the vascular plane, extending it from lateral to medial towards the midline. The duodenum is visible, and the fascia over the duodenum is carefully managed using a ligature device.

The surgeon extends the facial plane laterally, revealing the right ureter with a lighted stent, covered by fascia. Above the ureter is the colon mesentery. The right ileocolic vessel is visible, originating directly from the aorta. Two hemoclips are applied to the vessel for ligation.
The surgeon applies a lega clip and a hemoclip to the ileocolic vessel, then cuts the vessel between the two clips using a surgical instrument.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection, revealing the duodenum covered by a thin, papery peritoneal layer. A hole is made in this segment of the peritoneum, allowing further dissection along the side of the opening.

The surgeon continues dissection cranially and laterally, revealing the proximity of the ureter to the lateral wall of the duodenum. The facial plane is extended further, with small attachments being carefully dissected. The dissection proceeds between the xerotrophicia and the mesentery.

The surgeon begins meticulous dissection near the duodenum, carefully pushing down small vessels to prevent bleeding. The dissection is performed with precision to avoid organ injury, highlighting the delicate nature of this surgical step.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to dissect and seal tissue under direct visualization. The serotus fascia and the facial plane between the duodenum and the mesentery are clearly visible. The dissection progresses laterally, reaching the upper border of the duodenum.

The surgeon dissects the embryological plane between the Gerota's fascia and the right side mesentery, carefully separating the tissue layers. The dissection progresses further, with all visible fissures being taken down.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection towards the pancreatic head, identifying small vessels including the right branch of the middle colic vessel, which is ligated and cut using a ligature. Dense adhesions between the pancreatic head and surrounding structures are visible.
The surgeon uses a ligature to steadily cut through the mesentery, carefully managing small vessels to prevent bleeding during the dissection.

The surgeon views the gallbladder from below, with the pancreatic head visible beneath. The mesentery is being dissected, focusing on the attachments around the bowel. The supracolic compartment is reached, and small attachments are being carefully managed using a ligature.

The surgeon completes the dissection, addressing adhesions between the gallbladder and mesentery. The dissection of the supracolic compartment begins, with adhesions to the liver bed being managed using a ligature. The hepatic flexure attachments are also addressed, resulting in the complete mobilization and dissection of the hepatic flexure of the colon.

The surgeon begins dissection on the lateral side at the cecum, using a harmonic scalpel to cut the lateral attachments of the right colon. The glistening structure identified as the right ureter is visible, with a catheter in place. The harmonic scalpel is used to complete the mobilization of the right side colon.
The surgeon identifies the kidney, ureter, and duodenum, and proceeds to dissect the terminal ileum mesentery using a Migasur device.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to cut through the terminal ileum mesentery, progressing towards the bowel wall, with visible separation of the mesentery from the bowel.

The surgeon prepares the transverse colon for resection by addressing small mesenteric attachments using a ligature. Fatty tissue around the designated cut site in the small bowel is managed, and ICG mapping is performed to ensure proper visualization of the area.

The surgeon completes the preparation for resection at the transverse colon site. ICG dye is administered, revealing the vascularity and line of demarcation on the colon. The left side is marked for resection, while the right side appears intact. A 60 mm purple Endo GIA stapler is used to transect the transverse colon.

The transverse colon is transected, revealing a clear difference in vascularity between the two ends; one end shows complete vascularity while the other shows none. The small bowel is also visible, with congestion observed at the cut portion, contrasting with the normal appearance of the rest of the bowel.

The surgeon observes the vascularity differences between the left and right sides of the colon, indicating the level of transection. The endo-GIA stapler, purple, is applied with slight angulation to transect the small bowel, and the stapler is fired.

The surgical specimen is placed on the right side of the patient. The surgeon mobilizes the small bowel to perform a side-to-side ileocolic anastomosis. Two stay sutures are placed at the transected ends of the ileum and transverse colon. An endo-GIA 60 mm purple stapler is used to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an enterotomy using a hook on both the small bowel and transverse colon sides. A 60 mm stapler is introduced, approximated, and fired to create the anastomosis. The staple line is inspected internally for hemostasis, confirming no bleeding. The enterotomy is then closed with a U-Zero V-Lock barb suture, completing the first layer closure.

The surgeon performs a full-thickness closure of the surgical site, followed by a second layer of suturing using the same suture material. The rent between the middle of the colon and the small bowel mesentery is closed with sutures to prevent internal herniation.

The surgeon uses continuous sutures to close the range, followed by ICG mapping to assess the anastomotic part of the bowel between the transverse colon and small bowel. The mapping shows well-maintained vascularity on both sides, indicating a healthy anastomosis.
A suprapubic transverse incision of approximately four centimeters is made, and a wound protector is introduced. The surgical specimen is then delivered through this small incision.
The surgeon closes the wound in layers following the completion of the colectomy procedure.

And this is the growth at the cecum. And started orals on third postoperative day, postoperative period was uneventful, discharged on fourth postoperative day. The histopathology report turned out to be nineteen nodes dissected. all free of tumors and the histopathology will suggest you have well-differentiated adenocarcinoma with T-N-O-M-O.
Thanks for watching. Thank you.
